name: El Escudo Pass Loop
name_original: Barrio Corrobárceno, Santiurde de Toranzo, Puerto de El Escudo, Puente Viesgo – ruta circular
description: Tackle the difficult 65.1-mile (104.8 km) Barrio Corrobárceno circular road cycling route, climbing 5269 feet through Cantabria's Valles Pas
region_intro: Road cycling around Puente Viesgo offers diverse landscapes within the scenic Toranzo Valley of Cantabria. The region features a low-mountain environment traversed by the River Pas, providing varied terrain for cyclists. Routes include quiet rural roads and former railway lines converted into paved greenways. This area presents options ranging from mostly flat paths to challenging ascents with significant elevation gain. Best road cycling routes around Puente Viesgo The most popular road cycling route is El Escudo Pass Loop, a 65.1 miles (104.8 km) trail that takes 5 hours 15 minutes to complete. This difficult route features significant elevation gain through a mountainous landscape. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is El Caracol and La Estranguada Climbs Loop, a difficult 42.8 miles (68.8 km) path. This route is known for its challenging climbs and varied terrain. Local road cyclists also love the Pas and Besaya Greenways Loop, a 48.1 miles (77.4 km) trail leading through river valleys and greenways, often completed in about 4 hours 18 minutes. Road cycling around Puente Viesgo is defined by river valleys, low-mountain environments, and quiet rural roads. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult routes, catering to various ability levels. The routes in Puente Viesgo are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 80 reviews. More than 6,500 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Puente Viesgo's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Puente Viesgo?
A: Puente Viesgo offers a wide variety of road cycling routes, with over 150 options available. These routes cater to different preferences, including relaxed rides, challenging climbs, and scenic tours through the Toranzo Valley.

Q: Are there easy road cycling routes suitable for beginners or families?
A: Yes, Puente Viesgo is home to the &lt;strong&gt;Vía Verde del Pas (Pas Greenway)&lt;/strong&gt;, a former railway line converted into a fully paved, traffic-free path. This 14 km route is mostly flat and ideal for all ages and skill levels, including families and beginners. There are also over 20 other easy routes in the region.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes in Puente Viesgo?
A: The region offers diverse terrain, from the mostly flat and straight sections of the Vía Verde del Pas to quiet rural roads and challenging ascents in the low-mountain environment of the Toranzo Valley. You'll find routes with significant elevation gain, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1004914229/el-escudo-pass-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Escudo Pass Loop&lt;/a&gt;, and others that follow river valleys.

Q: What are some notable landmarks or natural features I can see along the cycling routes?
A: Many routes offer views of the scenic River Pas and the surrounding low-mountain landscapes. You can also explore significant historical sites like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4847953" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Quintana IV Tumulus&lt;/a&gt; or natural wonders such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4146541" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Churrón de Borleña Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;. The Monte Castillo Caves, famous for their prehistoric art, are also nearby.

Q: Are there any challenging routes for experienced road cyclists?
A: Absolutely. For experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1004914777/el-caracol-and-la-estranguada-climbs-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Caracol and La Estranguada Climbs Loop&lt;/a&gt; offer significant elevation gain and varied terrain. The region features nearly 50 difficult routes, with some extending over 100 km and climbing more than 2,600 meters.

Q: Can I find loop routes for road cycling in Puente Viesgo?
A: Yes, many road cycling routes in Puente Viesgo are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1004245984/pas-and-besaya-greenways-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pas and Besaya Greenways Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which combines scenic greenways with river valley cycling.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Puente Viesgo?
A: Puente Viesgo offers good cycling conditions for much of the year. Spring and autumn generally provide pleasant temperatures and beautiful scenery. Summer is also popular, especially for those who enjoy warmer weather, though it's always wise to check local forecasts.

Q: Are there places to stop for refreshments or breaks along the routes?
A: Yes, especially along popular routes like the Vía Verde del Pas, you'll find villages and recreational areas that offer opportunities for breaks and refreshments. The region's towns and villages often have cafes and restaurants where cyclists can refuel.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about cycling in Puente Viesgo?
A: The komoot community highly rates the road cycling experience in Puente Viesgo, with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 80 reviews. Cyclists often praise the diverse landscapes, from serene river valleys to challenging mountain passes, and the variety of routes catering to all skill levels.

Q: Where can I find viewpoints with scenic vistas?
A: The low-mountain environment of the Toranzo Valley provides numerous opportunities for scenic views. Specific viewpoints include &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3597279" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto de La Montaña&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4073927" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Viewpoint of Alto de la Cigüela&lt;/a&gt;, which offer panoramic vistas of the surrounding Cantabrian landscape.

Q: Is bike rental available in Puente Viesgo?
A: Yes, bike rentals are readily available in Puente Viesgo, making it convenient for visitors to access the popular Vía Verde del Pas and other local routes without bringing their own equipment.

Q: Are there any historical sites or cultural attractions accessible by bike?
A: Beyond the natural beauty, you can cycle past the beautifully preserved old Puente Viesgo Train Station, which sometimes hosts art exhibitions. The region is also known for the Monte Castillo Caves, a UNESCO World Heritage site with prehistoric cave art, offering a unique cultural stop.
tips: Also known as El Puente Rual, the aqueduct is next to the Vía Verde del Pas. It was built in 1885 in order to supply drinking water to the city of Santander. It is an ideal place to take a short break – there is shade, benches and tables. In front there is a bar and a fountain.

Within the municipality of Luena, the port of El Escudo is just over 15 kilometers long, and communicates with the neighboring province of Burgos. After an initial eight kilometers of false flat, the physiognomy changes completely. In the last seven kilometers, there is an average slope close to 9%, with peaks that reach 15%. It is a first class port.
Nice road bike route bordering the Ebro reservoir, Concorte, Cantabria.
Lantueno is a crossing point between Torrelavega and Reinosa with a natural and fresh water source ideal for recovering liquid.

German-made locomotive that entered service in 1913 to transport iron ores and limestone from the Camargo mines to its factory. It is located on the greenway, near the train station, converted into an exhibition hall and tourist information office.

faqs.question: What is the typical duration for completing this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: This challenging route, spanning approximately 105 kilometers with significant elevation gain, typically takes around 5 hours and 16 minutes to complete for an experienced cyclist. Your actual time may vary based on your fitness level and stops.

faqs.question: Is this circular road cycling route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this route is categorized as 'difficult' due to its length (105 km) and substantial elevation gain (1,610 meters). It includes demanding ascents and is best suited for experienced road cyclists with good endurance and climbing ability.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this cycling route?
faqs.answer: While there isn't one designated trailhead parking, you can typically find parking in the villages along the route, such as Barrio Corrobárceno, Santiurde de Toranzo, or Puente Viesgo. Look for public parking areas within these towns.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain will I encounter on this route?
faqs.answer: The route primarily consists of asphalt (97.2 km) and paved tracks (7.39 km), ensuring a smooth ride for road bikes. You'll be cycling on national roads for a significant portion (71.6 km), along with regular roads, bike paths, and some street sections.

faqs.question: What are the best times of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The Valles Pasiegos region, where this route is located, is generally best enjoyed during spring, summer, and early autumn. These seasons offer more favorable weather conditions for cycling, with less chance of extreme cold or snow, especially at higher elevations like El Escudo Pass.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or fees required to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this public road cycling route in Cantabria, Spain.

faqs.question: What notable sights or landmarks can I see along the way?
faqs.answer: The route offers picturesque landscapes and passes by several interesting points. You can see the Santiurde de Toranzo Aqueduct, traverse the challenging El Escudo Pass, and enjoy expansive views from the Viewpoint of the Ebro Reservoir. Other highlights include the Lantueno Spring and the Reyerma Steam Locomotive.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: As this is a road cycling route primarily on public roads, it is not typically considered 'dog-friendly' in the same way a hiking trail might be. While dogs on a leash are generally allowed in public spaces, cycling with a dog on a busy road for 105 km is not recommended for their safety or yours.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with any other well-known trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of this route overlap with several other paths. You will encounter parts of the Vía Verde del Pas, the Sendero Histórico: Cantabria, and the Camino del Besaya: Cantabria, among others.

faqs.question: Are there places to find food, drinks, or accommodation along the route?
faqs.answer: While specific amenities directly on the route might be limited, the broader Valles Pasiegos area, through which the route passes, offers various accommodations and eateries in its charming villages. You can find places to refuel and recover in towns like Santiurde de Toranzo and Puente Viesgo.

faqs.question: What should I bring for a day of cycling on this route?
faqs.answer: Given the route's length and difficulty, it's essential to be well-prepared. Bring plenty of water, energy snacks, a repair kit for your bike, appropriate cycling attire, sun protection, and a fully charged phone. A GPS device or the komoot app with the route downloaded is also highly recommended.
